title: Vertical datum

infobox.value: or **altimetric datum** is a reference [coordinate surface](/wiki/Coordinate_surface "Coordinate surface") used for [vertical positions](/wiki/Vertical_position "Vertical position"), such as the [elevations](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") of Earth-bound features ([terrain](/wiki/Terrain "Terrain"), [bathymetry](/wiki/Bathymetry "Bathymetry"), [water level](/wiki/Water_level "Water level"), and built structures) and [altitudes](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude") of [satellite orbits](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit#Altitude_classifications "Geocentric orbit") and in [aviation](/wiki/Altitude_in_aviation "Altitude in aviation").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Reference surface for vertical positions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Vertical_references_in_Europe.svg/250px-Vertical_references_in_Europe.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Vertical_references_in_Europe.svg)

Vertical datums in Europe

In [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy"), [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying"), [hydrography](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography") and [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"), **vertical datum** or **altimetric datum** is a reference [coordinate surface](/wiki/Coordinate_surface "Coordinate surface") used for [vertical positions](/wiki/Vertical_position "Vertical position"), such as the [elevations](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") of Earth-bound features ([terrain](/wiki/Terrain "Terrain"), [bathymetry](/wiki/Bathymetry "Bathymetry"), [water level](/wiki/Water_level "Water level"), and built structures) and [altitudes](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude") of [satellite orbits](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit#Altitude_classifications "Geocentric orbit") and in [aviation](/wiki/Altitude_in_aviation "Altitude in aviation").
In [planetary science](/wiki/Planetary_science "Planetary science"), vertical datums are also known as **zero-elevation surface**[[1]](#cite_note-Smith_Zuber_Solomon_Phillips_pp._1495–1503-1) or **zero-level reference**.[[2]](#cite_note-Smith_Zuber_pp._4397–4400-2)

Commonly adopted criteria for a vertical datum include the following approaches:

* [Tides](/wiki/Tides "Tides"), based on [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") when specific conditions occur, such as [NOAA](/wiki/NOAA "NOAA")'s [National Geodetic Survey](/wiki/National_Geodetic_Survey "National Geodetic Survey")-produced [tidal datums](/wiki/Tidal_datum "Tidal datum");
* [Gravimetric](/wiki/Gravimetric "Gravimetric"), based on a [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"); or geometric, based on the same [Earth ellipsoids](/wiki/Earth_ellipsoid "Earth ellipsoid") that are used in computing a horizontal datum, such as NOAA's planned gravimetric and [Global Navigation Satellite Systems](/wiki/Global_Navigation_Satellite_Systems "Global Navigation Satellite Systems") (GNSS)-based [Datum of 2022](/wiki/Datum_of_2022 "Datum of 2022") set to be released that year by the National Geodetic Survey.

In the United States, prominent vertical datums for professional uses include the [National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929](/wiki/National_Geodetic_Vertical_Datum_of_1929 "National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929") and the [North American Vertical Datum of 1988](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988").

## Methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_datum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Methods")]

In common usage, elevations are often cited in height above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"), although what "sea level" actually means is a more complex issue than might at first be thought: the height of the sea surface at any one place and time is a result of numerous effects, including waves, wind and currents, atmospheric pressure, [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide"), topography, and differences in the strength of gravity due to the presence of nearby ice sheets, mountains, and density variations in the crust and deep in the mantle.

For the purpose of measuring the height of objects on land, the usual datum used is [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") (MSL). This is a tidal datum which is described as the arithmetic mean of the hourly water elevation taken over a specific 19 years cycle. This definition averages out tidal highs and lows (caused by the gravitational effects of the sun and the moon) and short term variations. It will not remove the effects of local gravity strength, and so the height of MSL, relative to a geodetic datum, will vary around the world, and even around one country. Countries tend to choose the mean sea level at one specific point to be used as the standard "sea level" for all mapping and surveying in that country. (For example, in Great Britain, the national vertical datum, [Ordnance Datum Newlyn](/wiki/Ordnance_Datum_Newlyn "Ordnance Datum Newlyn"), is based on what was mean sea level at [Newlyn](/wiki/Newlyn "Newlyn") in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") between 1915 and 1921).[[3]](#cite_note-OSGB-3) However, zero elevation as defined by one country is not the same as zero elevation defined by another (because MSL is not the same everywhere), which is why locally defined vertical datums differ from one another.

A different principle is used when choosing a datum for [nautical charts](/wiki/Nautical_charts "Nautical charts"). For safety reasons, a mariner must be able to know the minimum depth of water that could occur at any point. For this reason, depths and tides on a nautical chart are measured relative to [chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum"), which is defined to be a level below which tide rarely falls. Exactly how this is chosen depends on the tidal regime in the area being charted and on the policy of the hydrographic office producing the chart in question; a typical definition is Lowest Astronomical Tide (the lowest tide predictable from the effects of gravity), or Mean Lower Low Water (the average lowest tide of each day), although MSL is sometimes used in waters with very low tidal ranges.

Conversely, if a ship is to safely pass under a low bridge or overhead power cable, the mariner must know the minimum clearance between the masthead and the obstruction, which will occur at high tide. Consequently, bridge clearances etc. are given relative to a datum based on high tide, such as Highest Astronomical Tide or Mean High Water Springs.

Sea level does not remain constant throughout [geological time](/wiki/Geological_time "Geological time"), and so tidal datums are less useful when studying very long-term processes. In some situations sea level does not apply at all – for instance for [mapping Mars' surface](/wiki/Geography_of_Mars "Geography of Mars") – forcing the use of a different "zero elevation", such as mean radius.

A geodetic vertical datum takes some specific zero point, and computes elevations based on the geodetic model being used, without further reference to sea levels. Usually, the starting reference point is a tide gauge, so at that point the geodetic and tidal datums might match, but due to sea level variations, the two scales may not match elsewhere. An example of a gravity-based geodetic datum is [NAVD88](/wiki/NAVD88 "NAVD88"), used in North America, which is referenced to a point in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). Ellipsoid-based datums such as [WGS 84](/wiki/WGS_84 "WGS 84"), [GRS80](/wiki/GRS80 "GRS80") or [NAD83](/wiki/NAD83 "NAD83") use a theoretical surface that may differ significantly from the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid").

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_datum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types")]

Common types of vertical datums include:[[4]](#cite_note-Taylor2002-4)

* The surface of the datum ellipsoid, resulting in an *[ellipsoidal height](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_height "Ellipsoidal height")*, *h*.
* The mean sea level as described by the gravity [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), yielding the *[orthometric height](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height")*, *H*.[[3]](#cite_note-OSGB-3)[[5]](#cite_note-USDOD-5)

Along with the [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") φ and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") λ, the ellipsoidal height h provides the three-dimensional *[geodetic coordinates](/wiki/Geodetic_coordinates "Geodetic coordinates")* (or *geographic coordinates*) for a location.[[6]](#cite_note-Kowk-6)

So, to completely specify a location of a topographical feature on, in, or above the Earth, one also has to specify the vertical position. The Earth is not a sphere, but an irregular shape approximating a [biaxial ellipsoid](/wiki/Earth_ellipsoid "Earth ellipsoid"). It is nearly spherical, but has an equatorial bulge making the radius at the Equator about 0.3% larger than the radius measured through the poles. The shorter axis approximately coincides with the axis of rotation. Though early navigators thought of the sea as a horizontal surface that could be used as a vertical datum, this is not actually the case. The Earth has a series of layers of equal [potential energy](/wiki/Potential_energy "Potential energy") within its [gravitational field](/wiki/Gravitational_field "Gravitational field"). Height is a measurement at right angles to this surface, roughly toward the Earth's center, but local variations make the equipotential layers irregular (though roughly ellipsoidal). The choice of which layer to use for defining height is arbitrary.

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_datum&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Examples")]

* Australia: [Australian Height Datum](/wiki/Australian_Height_Datum "Australian Height Datum")
* Austria, Albania and former [Yugoslavian](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") republics: [Metres above the Adriatic](/wiki/Metres_above_the_Adriatic "Metres above the Adriatic")
* France: [General levelling of France](/wiki/General_levelling_of_France "General levelling of France")
* Germany: [Normalhöhennull](/wiki/Normalh%C3%B6hennull "Normalhöhennull"), preceded by [Normalnull](/wiki/Normalnull "Normalnull")
* Great Britain: [Ordnance Datum Newlyn](/wiki/Ordnance_Datum_Newlyn "Ordnance Datum Newlyn")
* Netherlands: [Amsterdam Ordnance Datum](/wiki/Amsterdam_Ordnance_Datum "Amsterdam Ordnance Datum"), was also used by [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")
* Switzerland: [Metres above the Sea](/wiki/Metres_above_the_Sea_(Switzerland) "Metres above the Sea (Switzerland)")
* United States: [National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929](/wiki/National_Geodetic_Vertical_Datum_of_1929 "National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929"), [North American Vertical Datum of 1988](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988"), and the difference of the two in [VERTCON](/wiki/VERTCON "VERTCON")
* Global (geoid): [Earth Gravitational Model](/wiki/Earth_Gravitational_Model "Earth Gravitational Model") (EGM), EIGEN-6C4 and others[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Chart datums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vertical_datum&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chart datums")]

This section is an excerpt from [Chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chart_datum&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Tide_legal_use.gif/500px-Tide_legal_use.gif)](/wiki/File:Tide_legal_use.gif)

U.S. civil and maritime uses of tidal data

A [chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum") is the [water](/wiki/Water_level "Water level") surface serving as [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)") (or [coordinate surface](/wiki/Coordinate_surface "Coordinate surface")) of [depths](/wiki/Depth_(coordinate) "Depth (coordinate)") displayed on a [nautical chart](/wiki/Nautical_chart "Nautical chart") and for reporting and predicting [tide heights](/wiki/Tide_height "Tide height"). A chart datum is generally derived from some [tidal phase](/wiki/Tidal_phase "Tidal phase"), in which case it is also known as a tidal datum.[[8]](#cite_note-Chart_datum_bom-8) Common chart datums are *lowest astronomical tide* (LAT)[[8]](#cite_note-Chart_datum_bom-8) and *mean lower low water* (MLLW). In non-tidal areas, e.g., the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"), [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") (MSL) is used.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

A chart datum is a type of vertical datum and must not be confused with the [horizontal datum](/wiki/Horizontal_datum "Horizontal datum") for the chart.
However, it is *not* necessarily an [equigeopotential](/wiki/Equigeopotential "Equigeopotential") (a water "level surface"): the chart datum is tilted across smaller to larger [tidal range](/wiki/Tidal_range "Tidal range") regions; in rivers, it is a sloping and undulating surface following the low [stage](/wiki/Stage_(hydrology) "Stage (hydrology)").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
